coredata.dc:title: Graphene acid quantum dots: A highly active multifunctional carbon nano material that intervene in the trajectory towards neurodegeneration
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: ElMorsy, Sherin M.
Gutierrez, Denisse A
Valdez, Salvador
Kumar, Jyotish
Aguilera, Renato J
Noufal, Mohamed
Sarma, Hemen
Chinnam, Sampath
Narayan, Mahesh
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nano materials (CNMs) that find use across several biological applications because of their water solubility, biocompatible nature, eco-friendliness, and ease of synthesis. Additionally, their physiochemical properties can be chemically tuned for further optimization towards specific applications. Here, we investigate the efficacy of C70-derived Graphene Acid Quantum Dots (GAQDs) in mitigating the transformation of soluble, monomeric Hen Egg-White Lysozyme (HEWL) to mature fibrils during its amyloidogenic trajectory. Our findings reveal that GAQDs exhibit dose-dependent inhibition of HEWL fibril formation (up to 70 % at 5 mg/mL) without affecting mitochondrial membrane potential or inducing apoptosis at the same density. Furthermore, GAQDs scavenged reactive oxygen species (ROS); achieving a 50 % reduction in ROS levels at a mere 100 µg/mL when exposed to a standard free radical generator. GAQDs were not only found to be biocompatible with a human neuroblastoma-derived SHSY-5Y cell line but also rescued the cells from rotenone-induced apoptosis. The GAQD-tolerance of SHSY-5Y cells coupled with their ability to restitute cells from rotenone-dependent apoptosis, when taken in conjunction with the biocompatibility data, indicate that GAQDs possess neuroprotective potential. The data position this class of CNMs as promising candidates for resolving aberrant cellular outputs that associate with the advent and progress of multifactorial neurodegenerative disorders including Parkinson’s (PD) and Alzheimer’s diseases (AD) wherein environmental causes are implicated (95 % etiology). The data suggest that GAQDs are a multifunctional carbon-based sustainable nano-platform at the intersection of nanotechnology and neuroprotection for advancing green chemistry-derived, sustainable healthcare solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene acid quantum dots
Amyloid fibrils
Carbon nanomaterials
Reactive oxygen species
Biological imaging
Parkinson’s disease

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Texas at El Paso

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Mahesh Narayan acknowledges support from NIH 5R16GM145575-02 for this work. The work also required facilities of the Border Biomedical Research Center at The University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). We are also thankful to all the staff members of the cellular Characterization and Biorepository Core facility, Border Biomedical Research Center, the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP) supported by Grant U54MD007592.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical amyloidogenic trajectory showing the conversion of lysozyme into fibrils and the putative role of GAQDs on this mechanism.

(a) Schematic representation of the GAQDs synthesis using C70. (b) Powder X-ray diffraction of C70 and GAQDs. (c) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of as-prepared GAQDs. (d) HRTEM of the GAQDs (Inset represents the size distribution of the particles). (e) Excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of GAQDs.
Mitochondrial Membrane Potential Assay as a function of GAQD (Untreated and Positive Controls also shown).

(A–E) Flow cytometry density dot plots obtained to quantify values of apoptosis and necrosis induction. The bottom left quadrant represents healthy cells (−PI and −FITC). The quadrant on the bottom right shows the percentages of cells in early apoptosis (+FITC), population on the top right quadrant reflects cells in late apoptosis (+FITC, + PI), and top left quadrant indicates a necrotic population of cells (+PI). Colors in the plots represent a density gradient; grey being a low-density region and red high-density. GAQD impact on apoptosis. F) The apoptosis assay was performed using the SH-SY5Y cell line, 24 hrs post GAQD (2.5 and 5 mg/ml) exposure. To confirm that GAQDs do not induce apoptosis, phosphatidylserine externalization was measured in SH-SY5Y cells incubated with GAQDs (24 hrs; 2.5 and 5 mg/ml), PBS (vehicle), and H2O2 (apoptosis inducer) using the Annexin-V-FITC/PI assay. No significant apoptosis values were observed in cells treated with GAQDs (2.5 mg/ml = 5.9 % and 5 mg/ml = 3.8 %) in comparison with the vehicle control (PBS; 3.7 %). The gathered data suggest that GAQDs do not induce apoptosis up to the concentrations tested and therefore are not cytotoxic to the extent examined. Therefore, GAQDs can safely be used in human cells at concentrations ≤5 mg/ml as shown in Fig. 4a–f. Vehicle (PBS), untreated and hydrogen peroxide (+ve control) are shown. No significant apoptosis was observed in GAQDs-treated cells compared to the vehicle control. The asterisk annotation represents statistical significance of the data (***) p < 0.001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

HEWL fibril-forming assay. (a) GAQD dose dependent ThT fluorescence intensity (b) CD spectra of the fibrils as a function of GAQDs dose. (c) GAQD dose-dependent radical scavenging ability using the DPPH assay.

head.ce:title: Graphene acid quantum dots: A highly active multifunctional carbon nano material that intervene in the trajectory towards neurodegeneration

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Dept. of Chemistry and Biochemistry, UTEP, 500 W. University Ave., El Paso, TX 79968, United States.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nano materials (CNMs) that find use across several biological applications because of their water solubility, biocompatible nature, eco-friendliness, and ease of synthesis. Additionally, their physiochemical properties can be chemically tuned for further optimization towards specific applications. Here, we investigate the efficacy of C70-derived Graphene Acid Quantum Dots (GAQDs) in mitigating the transformation of soluble, monomeric Hen Egg-White Lysozyme (HEWL) to mature fibrils during its amyloidogenic trajectory. Our findings reveal that GAQDs exhibit dose-dependent inhibition of HEWL fibril formation (up to 70 % at 5 mg/mL) without affecting mitochondrial membrane potential or inducing apoptosis at the same density. Furthermore, GAQDs scavenged reactive oxygen species (ROS); achieving a 50 % reduction in ROS levels at a mere 100 µg/mL when exposed to a standard free radical generator. GAQDs were not only found to be biocompatible with a human neuroblastoma-derived SHSY-5Y cell line but also rescued the cells from rotenone-induced apoptosis. The GAQD-tolerance of SHSY-5Y cells coupled with their ability to restitute cells from rotenone-dependent apoptosis, when taken in conjunction with the biocompatibility data, indicate that GAQDs possess neuroprotective potential. The data position this class of CNMs as promising candidates for resolving aberrant cellular outputs that associate with the advent and progress of multifactorial neurodegenerative disorders including Parkinson’s (PD) and Alzheimer’s diseases (AD) wherein environmental causes are implicated (95 % etiology). The data suggest that GAQDs are a multifunctional carbon-based sustainable nano-platform at the intersection of nanotechnology and neuroprotection for advancing green chemistry-derived, sustainable healthcare solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aging remains the primary risk factor for protein misfolding-associated diseases including Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s (PD), systemic lysozyme amyloidosis, and Huntington’s disease [1–5]. Features associated across the aforementioned neurodegenerative disorders include elevated levels of oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, the conversion of monomeric proteins such as α-synuclein (in PD), amyloid beta ((Aβ) in AD) to their insoluble aggregates, resulting in neuronal injury and the loss of dopaminergic (PD) and cholinergic (AD) neurons [6].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are CNMs that have found applications in the environment, in signaling and in biomedicine [7,8]. Their abundance and facile synthesis using super critical conditions qualify them as green-chemistry derived CNMs that are generated sustainably. GAQDs have been applied in electrocatalysis, self-powered biosensors, theranostics, sensing, and in biomedicine. For example, sp2 hybridization-rich GAQDs have found utility in scavenging reactive oxygen species and citric-acid-derived Se-sulfur-containing CQDs possess free radical scavenging ability. GQDs intervened the fibrillation and cytotoxicity due to human islet amyloid polypeptide [9]. GQDs also intervened in a rodent model of PD by inhibiting the soluble-to toxic fibril formation of α-synuclein and provoking the disaggregation of mature fibrils [1,10,11].

From the examples listed, carbon-based nanomaterials, including GAQDs, possess advantages over small molecules across various applications [12]. Unlike small molecules, GAQDs and other CNMs allows for facile tuning of their chemical architectures which is important for advancing next-gen carbon nano materials tailored via structure–activity-relationship analyses [10,11]. Here, we have addressed whether soluble, surface-functionalized GAQDs can intervene across independent milestones found in the trajectory to a PD-like phenotypic outcome. The synthetic method developed in this work is important for the advantage it provides via precise size control of graphene quantum dots, a feature that is not possible by the Hummers' method [13].

As aforementioned, aggregation-prone proteins such as Aβ, HEWL, islet amyloid precursor protein (IAPP), α-synuclein, and mutant Huntingtin protein (mHTT) undergo a soluble-to-toxic transitioning wherein their soluble, monomeric forms form mature fibrils [7]. During this process, the monomeric protein dimerizes, oligomerizes and forms increasingly complex structures including protofibrils before eventually forming the insoluble (mature) fibrils [14]. Oligomers are considered the most toxic in nature as they act as seeds by which the process propagates. The seeds also puncture neuronal cell membranes and disrupt homeostasis [7,15]. Though results across independent studies are somewhat in contrast to one another, it has been accepted for some time now that fibrils are non-toxic or perhaps relatively less toxic [7]. By comparison, oligomers are now considered pathological (Fig. 1 ).

Previous reports reveal that the accumulation of misfolded protein aggregates and elevated levels of oxidative stress synergistically interact leading to an exacerbation in the trajectory to neurodegeneration [16]. Here, we demonstrate that GAQDs intervene in the conversion of a model amyloid-forming protein by intervening in the path to fibril formation and are also able to mitigate the ROS levels [17–19]. As aforementioned since each aberrant process influences the other, the plural interventional nature of GAQDs is even more impactful than the sum of the parts. The results suggest the advance of a relatively novel form of sustainable GAQDs to intervene in amyloid-associated disorders [20–22]. In this study, graphene acid quantum dots (GAQDs) are evaluated for their capacity to alter amyloid-forming pathways and preserve intracellular balance under oxidative stress conditions. GAQDs successfully inhibited the conversion of soluble monomers into mature fibrils of hen egg white lysozyme and scavenged reactive oxygen species when challenged with a wide range of free radicals and hydroxyl generators. Additionally, GAQDs were preincubated with the neuroblastoma-derived SHSY-5Y cell line to assess their ability to prevent cell death induced by these stressors, showing no cytotoxicity at concentrations up to 5 mg/mL compared to untreated controls.

C70 was subjected to the cage-opening procedure by introduction into a solution containing conc. H2SO4, NaNO3, and KMnO4 as previously reported by the Hummer’s method [23,24]. Fig. 2 a shows the reaction process. Upon quenching the reaction, a clear-ish yellow solution containing a small amount of precipitate was detected. The solution was dialyzed upon which the commercial C70 particles crystallized to the helical form I, possessing an intense peak at 12.3° of 2θ which correlates with 200 reflections coupled with 20.8° of 2θ corresponding to 210 reflections. An incipient low intensity rise at 17.9° of 2θ is indicative of a small amount of the crystalline phase (in ordered modification of form I). By contrast, a significant part remains disordered. X-Ray diffractions (XRD) of the GAQDs after the oxidation clearly show the formation of graphene quantum dots by showing 26.5° which is related to the 002 plane of the GAQDs as shown in Fig. 2b. HRTEM results (Fig. 2c, d) further confirm the uniform distribution of the nanoparticle with size range between 10–15 nm as shown in Fig. 1d (Inset). The excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra are also displayed (Fig. 2e). The data indicate a shift in the fluorescence emission to longer wavelengths (red shift) as a function of excitation wavelength.

The excitation dependent emission reflects the presence of intra- and intermolecular differences in the GAQDs due to differences in the degree of functionalization [7]. Nevertheless, strong luminescence was evident (Fig. 2e).

Next, we examined the mitochondrial transmembrane potential (ΔΨm) of neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells exposed to GAQDs. Healthy cells maintain a mitochondrial inner membrane electric potential, which should prevent the cell from apoptosis [1,25,26]. SH-SY5Y cells were treated with GAQDs, PBS and H2O2, (the last two included as vehicle and positive control, respectively), prior to staining using JC-1 polychromatic dye and analysis via flow cytometry (Fig. 3 a–f). The dye is specific to the mitochondria where it accumulates forming red fluorescent aggregates on cells with healthy mitochondria (top left subpopulation; Fig. 3). When the mitochondrial potential is lost, a diminution in the red/green fl. intensity ratio is observed (bottom right subpopulation) [1,25,26]. As shown in Fig. 3 a–f, no significant mitochondrial depolarization was found Fig. 3. GAQD impact on SHSY5Y mitochondrial membrane potential (A) Mitochondrial membrane potential was evaluated after treating cells with 2.5 and 5 mg/mL of GAQD for 5 h (analyzed using flow cytometry). Vehicle (PBS), untreated and H2O2 controls were included. Asterisk annotation refers to statistical significance of the treatment w.r.t vehicle control (***) p < 0.001. (B-F) Typical flow cytometry density plots used to calculate the fraction of cells exhibiting mitochondrial depolarization in cells that were incubated with GAQDs as compared to PBS (vehicle control). As expected, cells exposed to H2O2 displayed the highest percentage of depolarization (p < 0.001; 3A, F). Therefore, our results demonstrate that GAQDs does not perturb mitochondrial health in SH-SY5Y cells and will most likely not induce apoptosis.

Fenton assay further confirmed the antioxidant activity of GAQDs to oxygenated radicals such as hydroxy groups as shown in Fig. S1 and discussed in supporting information. Fig. 4 a shows ThT fluorescence intensity trajectories depicting the binding of ThT to mature (HEWL) fibrils and the impact of GAQDs on the HEWL fibril formation. In the control experiment (magenta), ThT data revealed a strong growth curve in fluorescence intensity when the dye was introduced into mature fibrils. Saturation was achieved relatively quickly. By contrast, when HEWL monomers were preincubated with GAQDs (0.6–5 mg/mL) the ThT fluorescence growth curves were inhibited in a GAQD concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 5 a). The GAQD dose-dependent reduction in ThT fluorescence reflects the inhibition processes involved in the conversion of HEWL into mature fibrils. Under physiological conditions, the covalently bonded carbon core of GAQDs does not ionize into toxic species. Several GAQD surface atoms are neutral while some of the surface groups are polar in nature (δ−) as shown in Fig. S2. Furthermore, the GAQDs possess high biocompatibility (comparable to vehicle control even at high doses as shown in the cytotoxicity assay) [27]. Theoretically speaking, positively charged atoms are the most cytotoxic [27]. As aforementioned, our studies have shown no toxicity for GAQDs that are predominantly functionalized with oxygen-containing groups on their surfaces. Nevertheless, we have updated the discussion accordingly.

To underscore process(es) by which GAQDs interfere in the soluble monomer → mature fibril forming process, we examined the GAQDs-dependent 2° structure of HEWL by circular dichroism (CD). The CD data are indicative of a GAQD concentration-dependent decrease in the β-sheet structure of HEWL. As a function of increasing GAQD dose (0.65 → 5 mg/mL), a complete elimination of HEWL β-sheet content was observed (Fig. 5b) indicating inhibition of mature fibril formation via beta-sheet elimination (by GAQDs) [28–30].

The onset and progress of neurodegeneration is related to oxidative stress (elevated ROS) [1,25,26]. This pathway is a second mechanism by which neuronal injury leading to neuronal demise is initiated. We examined whether GAQDs can intervene to mitigate ROS levels associated with neurodegenerative onset. To address this, the DPPH assay was adopted to estimate the ROS scavenging capability of GAQDs. An examination of the data (Fig. 5c) clearly underscores the (dose-dependent) capacity of GAQDs to intervene by resetting ROS levels. GAQDs is an ideal antioxidant compared to previously reported Quantum dots. We highlighted this comparison in Table S1.

Here, we have synthesized GAQDs nanoparticles from C70 fullerenes. The CNMs were able to intervene in conversion of HEWL into fibrillar aggregates. Separately, the GAQDs were able to scavenge free radicals using an established assay for assessing antioxidant activity. Importantly, the GAQDs were found to be biocompatible up to 5 mg/ml. Together, the data underscore the ability of biocompatible GAQDs to perform independent interventional functions along the trajectory to amyloidogenesis. The GAQDs can prevent the toxic conversion of amyloid proteins, reset cellular dyshomeostasis by scavenging ROS, and rescue cells from mitochondrial poisoning-induced apoptosis (and necrotic cell death). These aspects position them as a potentially viable candidate to advance to further trials.

All materials and reagents were obtained from commercial sources. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (Fisher Chemical), C70 (Fisher Scientific, USA), Annexin V- FITC apoptosis kit (Abnova, USA), carboxy-H2DCFDA (Invitrogen, USA), quinine sulfate fluorescence (AnaSpec, USA), DPPH (Millipore-Sigma, USA), potassium persulfate (Fisher Chemical, UK), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (Acros Organics, USA), hen-egg white lysozyme (MP Biomedicals LLC, France), thioflavin T and rotenone (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Atlanta Biologicals, USA), Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) (Corning, USA), and SH-SY5Y (ATCC, USA). All reagents were of analytical grade.

The GAQDs were synthesized using the supercritical hydrothermal method after oxidation of C70 fullerenes using potassium permanganate, and sulfuric acid (3/1 v/v) was typically mixed and stirred for 10 min. Then, the graphene acid powder was added to this suspension under constant stirring. Material Characterization Structural characterization (XRD) of GAQDs was performed on a Bruker X-ray diffractometer with a Cu Kα X-ray source. For the HRTEM analysis, the samples were drop-casted onto a carbon-coated Cu grid and interpreted using an H9500 (HITACHI) HRTEM equipped with a LaB6 electron gun, operated with at 300 kV, resolution 0.1 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is calculated using the equation.
φ A
=
φ S
×
F A
F S
×
OD
S
OD
A
F: integrated fluorescence intensity; OD: absorbance at 360 nm; A and S: arrestin and standard, respectively. The QY of the standard quinine sulfate is 0.55. The relative QY of the GAQDs was estimated at 0.53 [7].

To underscore the potential of GAQDs to onset apoptosis was evaluated by measuring phosphatidylserine externalization in SH-SY5Y cells [1,25,26] 100,000 cells were seeded using 1 mL of culture media onto 24-well plates and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The next day, the following treatments were made GAQD 2.5 and 5 mg/mL, 1 % v/v PBS (vehicle), and 1 mM H2O2 (positive control of cell death). Cells were incubated at optimal conditions for 24 hrs and subsequently used to perform the Annexin-V-FITC/PI assay kit (Beckman Coulter, Miami, FL, USA) as per the manufacturer’s instructions. Cells were centrifuged at 262× g for 5 min, supernatants were decanted, and cells re-suspended 500 µl of PBS. Cells were spun down at 262× g for 5 min, and pellets were re-suspended in 100 µl of a mixture containing ice-cold 1X binding buffer, Annexin V-FITC and Propidium iodide (PI). Tubes were vortexed and incubated on ice in the dark for 30 min. After incubation, 300 µl of ice cold 1X binding buffer were added to each tube and readings were taken immediately using a Gallios flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter, Brea, CA, USA). Approximately 10,000 events/cells were acquired per sample and data analysis was achieved via the Kaluza 1.3 software (Beckman Coulter). To identify if GAQD induces mitochondrial damage in SH-SY5Y cells, the MitoProbe JC-1 assay kit (Molecular Probes, Eugene, OR, USA; M34152) and flow cytometry were utilized [31,32]. Cells were seeded at a density of 100,000 cells in 1 mL of culture media in 24-well plates and incubated overnight at optimal conditions (37 °C, 5 % CO2 atmosphere). The next day, cells were treated with 2.5 and 5 mg/mL of GAQDs, 1 % v/v PBS (vehicle control), and 1 mM H2O2 (oxidative stress inducer) and incubated for 5 hrs at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Afterwards, cells were harvested in flow cytometry tubes and centrifuged at 262× g for 5 min. Supernatants were decanted and cells re-suspended in 500 µl of PBS. Then, 5 µl of the JC-1 fluorescent dye (2 µM final concentration) were added to each tube and samples were incubated at 37 °C (5 % CO2 atmosphere) for 30 min. Cells were then washed by adding 1 mL of pre-warmed PBS to each tube and centrifuged at 262× g for 5 min. Cell pellets were re-suspended in 500 µl of PBS and immediately analyzed in a Gallios flow cytometer. FL1 and FL2 detectors were selected for identifying Alexa Fluor 488 and R-phycoerythrin in samples. On healthy cells, the JC-1 dye aggregates in the mitochondria due to the high membrane potential, emits a red signal. When the mitochondrial membrane potential is lost the JC-1 dye disassociates into monomers and emits a green signal. Approximately 10,000 events were collected per sample and the Kaluza 1.3 software was utilized for data analysis.

HEWL fibrils were generated by introducing ∼1–3 mg/mL of monomeric HEWL in a partially unfolding buffer (20 mM KH2PO4, 2 M guanidine hydrochloride; 60 °C, 500 rpm for 4 h in an incubator shaker (Multi-therm, Benchmark) [7]. In the treatment samples, differing concentrations of GAQDs were added prior to incubation. To assess the presence of fibrils, samples were placed in a Quartz cuvette and then 20 μM ThT was introduced. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a DM45 Olis spectrofluorometer (Exc.@450 nm, Em.@482 nm). Each experiment was repeated thrice.

For GAQDs (0–0.5 mg. mL−1) dependent secondary structural measurements, CD spectra were gathered at 25 °C using a bandwidth of 1 nm, a step interval of 1 nm, 50 nm min−1 scan rate (slit width of 0.02 mm; (model JASCO J-1500, USA). A quartz cell with a 0.1 mm path length was used (190–260 nm) for measurements. Different concentrations of GAQDs (0–5 mg. mL−1) were mixed with 1 mg. mL−1 HEWL in 5 mM Tris-HCl buffer. The GAQDs were added to the protein solution and incubated for 30 min prior to data collection. Scans in triplicate were measured and averaged. Control buffer scans were averaged and subtracted from the sample spectra.

A DPPH solution (0.2 mg/mL0 was prepared in 200 proof ethanol and stored (4 °C). Ascorbic acid was the +ve control. Different concentrations of GAQDs (10–100 μg/mL prepared as a stock in ethanol) were added to individual vials containing DPPH. Vials were kept dark for 30 min for the reaction to proceed prior to obtaining UV/Vis data (517 nm).

The percentage scavenging activity was calculated as follows [1,25,26]:
% A n t i o x i d a n t A c t i v i t y =
AbsorbanceofDPPH - A b s o r b a n c e o f S a m p l e
AbsorbanceofDPPH

Sherin M. ElMorsy: Formal analysis, Data curation. Denisse A Gutierrez: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Salvador Valdez: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jyotish Kumar: Formal analysis, Data curation. Renato J Aguilera: Formal analysis. Mohamed Noufal: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hemen Sarma: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Sampath Chinnam: Writing – review & editing. Mahesh Narayan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Mahesh Narayan acknowledges support from NIH 5R16GM145575-02 for this work. The work also required facilities of the Border Biomedical Research Center at The University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP). We are also thankful to all the staff members of the cellular Characterization and Biorepository Core facility, Border Biomedical Research Center, the University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP) supported by Grant U54MD007592.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.05.072.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
